name: James Baldwin
abstract: James Arthur Baldwin (né Jones; August 2, 1924 – December 1, 1987) was an American writer and civil rights activist who garnered acclaim for his essays, novels, plays, and poems. His 1953 novel Go Tell It on the Mountain has been ranked by Time magazine as one of the top 100 English-language novels.

about_quotes.speaker: Jang
about_quotes.evidence: And I’ll say one more thing is that James Baldwin wrote a powerful response to James Lincoln Collier’s The Making of Jazz

about_quotes.speaker: Roy Haynes
about_quotes.evidence: I used to hang out with him a lot in the early days. It was a lot of closeness
about_quotes.doc_title: Roy Haynes — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Dick Griffin
about_quotes.evidence: Dr. King was eating lunch next door in a place called the College Inn, and he had a lot of celebrities like Harry Belafonte, Lena Horne, Sammy Davis Junior, James Baldwin, I saw all of those people in Jackson, when they came through Jackson.
about_quotes.doc_title: Dick Griffin interviewed by Monk Rowe, via Zoom, February 12, 2024

about_quotes.speaker: Greg Thomas
about_quotes.evidence: I once asked Albert Murray if James Baldwin was an influence on him. And he said, “Yes. He was an influence. He influenced me in what not to do.”
about_quotes.doc_title: Greg Thomas interviewed by Monk Rowe, Clinton, New York, October 11, 2014

about_quotes.speaker: Yusef Lateef
about_quotes.evidence: like the late James Baldwin said, “Beware of how people define you, define that which you do, because they may misrepresent you.”
about_quotes.doc_title: Yusef Lateef — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Stanley Dance
about_quotes.evidence: The one on Earl Hines had a full page review by James Baldwin, but it still was the least seller you know.
about_quotes.doc_title: Stanley Dance interviewed by Monk Rowe, San Diego, California, February 12, 1998
